coredata.dc:title: Enhancing blue phosphorescence in matrix-free carbon dots through covalent crosslinking
coredata.dc:creator: Shi, Huixian
Wu, Yang
Xu, Jiahui
Zhou, Chifeng
Xu, Hao
Ye, Wenpeng
Yin, Yanfei
Wang, Zhaoyu
Su, Ruifa
An, Zhongfu
Shi, Huifang
coredata.dc:description: Room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) of carbon dots (CDs) has received extensive attention due to its excellent performance and promising application. Here, a novel kind of matrix-free CDs with blue phosphorescence emission was synthesized by a simple one-step hydrothermal method of heating (3-Aminopropyl) trimethoxysilane (APTMS) and phosphoric acid in aqueous solution. The obtained CDs show a phosphorescence lifetime of 315.73 ms, and the absolute photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) and the phosphorescence quantum yield (PQY) can reach up to 37.8 % and 12.4 %, respectively. From the experimental results, we speculate that the generation of RTP is attributed to the restriction effect of the covalent crosslinking structure of SiOSi, SiOC, or SiC bonds formed on the CDs surface. In addition, phosphoric acid as a crosslinking agent and heteroatom dopant can promote the formation of cross-linked structures and facilitate the intersystem crossing (ISC) process, thus enhancing room temperature phosphorescence. Finally, the CDs were successfully used in information encryption and anti-counterfeiting.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Room temperature phosphorescence
Carbon spot
Covalent crosslinking
Information encryption

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of fluorescent CDs for application

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Structure characteristics of AP-CDs. (a) TEM image (inset: HR-TEM image). (b) Size distribution. (c) FT-IR spectrum. (d) XPS survey spectrum and high-resolution spectrum of (e) C 1 s and (f) Si 2p of the AP-CDs.

Photophysical characteristics of AP-CDs. (a) The UV–Vis absorption (black dotted line), steady-state photoluminescence (PL) excitation (blue line), and phosphorescence excitation (red line) spectra of the CDs. (b) Photoluminescence emission (black dotted line) and phosphorescence emission (red line) spectrum of AP-CDs powder under ambient conditions. (c) Corresponding CIE coordinates of photoluminescence and phosphorescence emission. (d) Time-resolved phosphorescence decay of the emission bands at 470 nm with 310 nm excitation. (e) Photographs of AP-CDs powder excited by 365 nm UV light and after switching off the UV light under ambient conditions. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Phosphorescence mechanism of AP-CDs. (a) The XPS survey spectra of AP-CDs and the control NP-CDs. (b) Time-resolved phosphorescence decay curves of AP-CDs and the control NP-CDs at 440 and 470 nm, respectively. (c) Corresponding CIE coordinates of phosphorescence emission of AP-CDs and the control NP-CDs. (d) Schematic diagram of the proposed possible limiting effect of covalent bonds and hydrogen bonds on chromophores on the surface of CDs. (e) Energy diagram of fluorescence and phosphorescence emission processes promoted by covalent crosslinking.

Anti-counterfeiting and information encryption applications. (a) Digital encryption demonstration realized by combining fluorescent and phosphorescent carbon dots. (b) Letters information encryption is made from AP-CDs and fluorescent CDs. (c) Photographs of Anti-counterfeiting application with a pattern under daylight, 365 nm UV lamp ON and OFF, respectively.
Schematic illustration of the preparation process of AP-CDs and corresponding photographs taken under daylight, 365 nm UV light on and off, respectively.

head.ce:title: Enhancing blue phosphorescence in matrix-free carbon dots through covalent crosslinking

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shanxi Key Laboratory of Biomedical Metal Materials, College of Materials Science and Engineering, Taiyuan University of Technology, Taiyuan 030024, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shanxi Key Laboratory of Biomedical Metal Materials
College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shanxi Key Laboratory of Biomedical Metal Materials, College of Materials Science and Engineering, Taiyuan University of Technology, Taiyuan 030024, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Flexible Electronics (KLOFE) & Institute of Advanced Materials (IAM), Nanjing Tech University, 30 South Puzhu Road, Nanjing 211816, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Flexible Electronics (KLOFE) & Institute of Advanced Materials (IAM)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Flexible Electronics (KLOFE) & Institute of Advanced Materials (IAM), Nanjing Tech University, 30 South Puzhu Road, Nanjing 211816, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Blue phosphorescent carbon dots material was prepared by a simple hydrothermal method. The obtained materials showed a long lifetime of 315.73 ms and a phosphorescent quantum yield of 12.4%. It was found that the RTP originated from the restriction of the covalent crosslinking structure formed on the surface of carbon dots to chromophores. Furthermore, the carbon dots material has been successfully used in information encryption and anti-counterfeiting applications.

Room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) of carbon dots (CDs) has received extensive attention due to its excellent performance and promising application. Here, a novel kind of matrix-free CDs with blue phosphorescence emission was synthesized by a simple one-step hydrothermal method of heating (3-Aminopropyl) trimethoxysilane (APTMS) and phosphoric acid in aqueous solution. The obtained CDs show a phosphorescence lifetime of 315.73 ms, and the absolute photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) and the phosphorescence quantum yield (PQY) can reach up to 37.8 % and 12.4 %, respectively. From the experimental results, we speculate that the generation of RTP is attributed to the restriction effect of the covalent crosslinking structure of SiOSi, SiOC, or SiC bonds formed on the CDs surface. In addition, phosphoric acid as a crosslinking agent and heteroatom dopant can promote the formation of cross-linked structures and facilitate the intersystem crossing (ISC) process, thus enhancing room temperature phosphorescence. Finally, the CDs were successfully used in information encryption and anti-counterfeiting.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) materials, as a long-lived emission material, have been widely used in information encryption [1,2], anti-counterfeiting [3,4], chemical sensing [5], biological imaging [6–8], and electroluminescent devices [9,10] in recent years due to their rich excited state properties. However, due to the spin-forbidden and fast non-radiative transition characteristics of triplet exciton transitions and being extremely sensitive to external environments (such as oxygen and water), it is still challenging to achieve effective phosphorescent emission. At present, many phosphorescent materials, including inorganic materials based on rare earth ions and transition metals [11–13], organic metal complexes [14–16], and pure organic phosphorescent materials [17–20], have been developed. However, considering the high toxicity, expensive cost, low stability, and complex preparation conditions, it seriously restricts the application range of these material systems.

Carbon dots (CDs), as a new type of carbon-based nanomaterials, have attracted great attention and research in recent years due to their rich surface functional groups, excellent luminescence properties, good biocompatibility, low cost, low toxicity, and simple preparation process [21–23]. Notably, the emission color turnability of CDs has attracted considerable attention. As is known to all, blue light as one of the three primary colors plays an indispensable role in white-light solid-state lighting and full-color display [24–27]. For example, it is the core component of white light-emitting devices [28]. Therefore, the development of effective blue light emission has far-reaching significance for the development and application of CDs. Recently, great effort was devoted to develop CDs with colorful phosphorescence under ambient conditions. However, most reports focus on green and yellow luminescence [29–31]. Blue phosphorescence at room temperature is relatively difficult to achieve due to the difficulties in filling and stabilizing the triplet exciton at a high energy state [3]. Nevertheless, some significant progress has been made in blue-emitting CDs. Zheng et al. achieved blue-emitting RTP CDs with the QY of 26.36 % by embedding CDs in silica-based matrix with highly rigid network and stable covalent bonding [32]. Bai's group introduced a large number of (n, π*) electronic configurations into the CDs using the strategy of hot-urea-bath, and the prepared CDs composite with 50.17 % high phosphorescent quantum yield [33]. Recently, the high-efficiency temperature-responsive blue afterglow CDs composites have been realized by embedding the luminescent centers of ciprofloxacin into the formed rigid boric acid matrix [34]. Notably, these progresses are achieved through multi-step reactions to embed CDs with rigid matrices. The CDs will generate RTP emission only when they are coated with a specific matrix, which makes this strategy not universal. In addition, matrix-protected CDs composites usually require quadratic high-temperature heating, which requires that the CDs itself has good stability and may introduce other impurities during the preparation process. In matrix-free self-protected system, the introduction of heteroatoms (N, O, S, P, etc.) can promote the n-π* transitions of the chromophores, which can enhance the intersystem crossing (ISC) process for RTP improvement [35,36]. Yang and his co-authors designed a novel kind of metal-free phosphorescent afterglow materials thought hydrothermal method without matrices at room temperature. It is found that the RTP is associated with a crosslink-enhanced emission (CEE) effect [37]. This strategy provides a platform for the rational design of the matric-free afterglow materials. Therefore, by selecting appropriate precursor molecules, it is possible that the covalent cross-linking framework can be formed during the synthesis process of CDs to limit the vibration and rotation of chromophores on the surface of CDs, which can inhibit non-radiative transitions and achieve blue RTP emission of matrix-free CDs.

To valid our concept, we selected (3-Aminopropyl) trimethoxysilane (APTMS) with rich silicon-oxygen bonds as an ideal phosphorescent precursor and phosphoric acid as a crosslinking agent. Obviously, the covalent crosslinking restriction is likely to be formed at high temperatures [29,37]. Hence, the CDs named AP-CDs were synthesized by a one-pot hydrothermal method from APTMS and phosphoric acid (Scheme 1 ). The prepared matrix-free CDs material exhibits blue RTP with the PQY of 12.4 % and the phosphorescence lifetime of 315.73 ms, which is attributed to the covalent restriction of SiOSi, SiOC or SiC bonds.

4.0 mL of (3-Aminopropyl) trimethoxysilane (APTMS) was dissolved in 30 mL of deionized water, and then 1.0 mL of phosphoric acid was added into the aqueous solution of APTMS with magnetic stirring for 20 min. Next, the mixture solution was added to a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 220 °C for 10 h. After the reaction, the reactor was cooled down to room temperature naturally. Subsequently, the aqueous solution of the crude product was filtered by 0.22 μm membranes to remove impurities and agglomerated particles. The obtained solution was fully dialyzed in a dialysis bag (MWCO:1000 Da) for 3 days to remove some by-products and precursors of incomplete reaction. Finally, the white powder of AP-CDs was obtained by freeze-drying (Scheme S1).

Control experiments were prepared under similar reaction conditions as AP-CDs. The carbon dots prepared with n-butylamine instead of APTMS and with no phosphoric acid added as precursors were named NP-CDs and A-CDs, respectively (Scheme S2). Except for raw materials, all preparation steps and processes were strictly consistent with AP-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of fluorescent CDs for application
ce:section.ce:para: Typically, 0.5 g of oxamide was dissolved in 30 mL aqueous solution with an ultrasound device and stirred for 10 min until uniform. Next, the above solution is transferred to a 50 mL beaker, covered with a layer of tin foil, and heated at 180 °C for 5 h. After the reaction, the reactor was cooled down to room temperature naturally. Subsequently, dissolve the crude product in the beaker with 50 mL of deionized water and filtered with 0.22 μm membranes to remove impurities and agglomerated particles. The obtained solution was fully dialyzed in a dialysis bag (MWCO:500 Da) for 36 h to remove some by-products and precursors of incomplete reaction. Finally, the fluorescent CDs were obtained by freeze-drying (Scheme S3). All applications are carried out by filling the carbon dots powder in the pattern template.

After purified by filtration, dialysis, and freeze-drying, AP-CDs powder was obtained. As shown in Scheme 1, the white AP-CDs powder showed deep blue fluorescence and sky-blue phosphorescence before and after UV-light excitation, respectively.

To better understand the luminescent properties of AP-CDs, its structure was characterized comprehensively. The morphology of AP-CDs was observed by TEM. As shown in Fig. 1 a,1b and S2, the CDs are uniformly dispersed, showing the spherical shape and zero-dimensional characteristics with an average size of about 2.54 nm. The inset image of the high-resolution in Fig. 1a shows the CDs have an obvious lattice structure with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, which should belong to the (100) crystal plane of graphite, indicating the core structure of AP-CDs [38]. The functional groups and the corresponding chemical states on the surface of CDs were analyzed by FT-IR and XPS. The FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 1c) shows a wide absorption band range from 500 to 4000 cm−1. The absorption peaks at 3420 and 2940 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration of NH and CH bonds, respectively. Notably, the strong absorption peaks at 1640, 1043, and 763 cm−1 were determined to be caused by the stretching vibration of the C=N, SiO, and SiC groups, respectively, indicating the formation of covalent bonds on the surface of CDs [32,39]. In addition, the other three absorption peaks located at 1553, 1470, and 1219 cm−1 proved the presence of the stretching vibration with P=O, C-C, and C-O groups, respectively. In order to further confirm the surface functional groups, the XPS spectra were measured. As shown in Fig. 1d, the XPS survey spectrum indicates that AP-CDs are mainly composed of C, N, O, P, and Si. It is worth noting that EDS mapping (Fig. S3 and S4) shows that the content of the Si element of the CDs reaches 23.3 %, which obviously helps to form covalent interactions, such as Si–O and SiC. The existence of SiC (282.9 eV), C=N (283.8 eV), C-C (284.9 eV), and C–O (285.9 eV) groups were further confirmed from the high-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1 s (Fig. 1e) [29]. The XPS Si 2p spectrum (Fig. 1f) shows binding energy at 100.7, 101.3, and 101.8 eV that can be attributed to SiC, SiOSi, and SiOC bonds, respectively [39,40]. Moreover, the XPS N 1 s spectrum can be divided into three peaks of N–H, N–C3, and C–N=C, which are located at 398.3, 399.9, and 400.8 eV, respectively (Fig. S5a). Additionally, the XPS O 1 s spectrum contains three bands that can be assigned to P=O, C-O, and Si-O with the binding energy of 528.9, 530.2, and 531.3 eV, respectively (Fig. S5b). The XPS P 2p spectrum shows the presence of O=P–O (131.7 eV) and O=P-N (132.6 eV) groups (Fig. S5c) [41]. By analyzing the above data, it is found that the FT-IR and XPS results are highly consistent, which indicates that there are indeed abundant chemical functional groups on the surface of AP-CDs. It is noteworthy that C=N groups are formed on the surface of the CDs at high temperature, which have been proven to be phosphorescent emitters [36]. The strong spin–orbit coupling can effectively promote the intersystem crossing (ISC), which is benefit to realize the phosphorescence. Besides, the covalent bonds, formed on the surface of the CDs through carbonization, polymerization, and crosslinking, play a key role in limiting the non-radiative dissipation of C=N chromophores

Subsequently, the photophysical properties of AP-CDs were studied. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the normalized UV–vis absorption spectrum of the AP-CDs shows that the absorption band is mainly in the ultraviolet region. The absorption peaks at 295 and 380 nm are attributed to the π-π* and n-π* transitions of C=C, C-C, C-O, and C=N groups on the surface of the CDs, respectively [23,29]. The overlap of the UV–vis absorption, PL excitation, and phosphorescence excitation spectra indicates that the luminescence should come from the sub-fluorophores and phosphorescent emitters contained in the CDs. The AP-CDs powder shows deep blue emission under 365 nm UV light excitation with the emission peak at 432 nm, of which the emission lifetime is 6.1 ns (Fig. S6). As temperature increasing from 80 to 400 K, the lifetimes of the emission at 432 nm gradually decrease, indicating there is no thermal activated delayed fluorescence. When the UV light excitation is removed, the AP-CDs exhibits sky blue emission with a peak at 470 nm (Fig. 2b). The Δ EST between the lowest triplet (T1) and the lowest singlet gap was calculated to be is 0.23 eV. Such a relative low value benefits effective ISC process to populate triplet excitons [33]. Notably, phosphorescence spectra show excitation-independent behaviors (Fig. S7), which suggests that a single emission cluster is formed on the AP-CDs surface [41,42]. As shown in Fig. 2c, it can be clearly observed that PL and phosphorescence emission of AP-CDs are located in the blue area with the corresponding Commission International de l'Eclairage (CIE) coordinates of (0.181, 0.127) and (0.196, 0.190), respectively. Furthermore, the absolute PL quantum yield (PLQY) of AP-CDs powder is about 37.8 % under an optimum excitation wavelength of 370 nm. By calculating the overlap ratio of PL and phosphorescence spectra, we obtained that the phosphorescence quantum yield (PQY) of AP-CDs powder is 12.4 %, which is a relatively high level in the reported matrix-free blue phosphorescence CDs system (most are less than 10 %) (Fig. S8) [41,43–46] .

In order to further understand the RTP properties of AP-CDs, we measured its time-resolved phosphorescence decay lifetime, which can be fitted by a double exponential function. The lifetimes are 28.09 and 315.73 ms, respectively (Fig. 2d and Table S1). These results indicate that the AP-CDs have multiple lifetime decay channels. According to the following equation [29,32]:
τ
avg
= ∑
α i
τ i 2
/ ∑
α i
τ i

The calculated average lifetime is 218.76 ms. Moreover, by taking the phosphorescence photos of AP-CDs after removing the excitation of 365 nm ultraviolet light, the blue emission of about 5.5 s can be observed by the naked eyes (Fig. 2e). As decay time extends, it is found the profiles of the phosphorescence spectra are fixed, indicating there is no time dependent phosphorescence (Fig. S9). Moreover, As shown in Fig. S10, the phosphorescence was stable under oxygen due to their compacted CDs structure that can effectively prevent oxygen.

To deeply explore and understand the RTP emission mechanism of AP-CDs, some control experiments were conducted. We speculate that the rich silicon-oxygen bonds in precursors are benefit to form covalent cross-linked structures on the surface of CDs, thereby limiting the rotation and vibration of chromophores. Moreover, the crosslinking agent may effectively connect adjacent oligomers during carbonization and polymerization, thereby promoting the formation of covalent crosslinking structure [29,41]. Hence, we chose n-butylamine to replace APTMS and removed phosphoric acid to synthesize control CDs (named NP-CDs and A-CDs, respectively) under the same experimental conditions. NP-CDs and A-CDs both show the same quasi-spherical morphology as AP-CDs with sizes of 3.56 nm and 4.41 nm, respectively (Fig. S11). The slight increase in size may be due to the lack of cross-linking effect of phosphoric acid, and the distance between adjacent oligomers cannot be pulled in during the carbonization polymerization process. As shown in Fig. 3 a, compared with AP-CDs, there is no Si-related photoelectron characteristic peak in the XPS survey spectrum of NP-CDs, indicating the covalent cross-linking restriction does not form on the surface of NP-CDs. The FT-IR and corresponding XPS high-resolution spectra of NP-CDs and A-CDs also proves it (Fig. S12-S14). Similarly, the covalent structure can also form, but not enough rigid and tight without the connection of phosphoric acid between the adjacent oligomers Notably, lacking the doping of P, the n-π* transitions of emitters such as C=N are inhibited. It is not conducive to the occurrence of the ISC process and leads to the decline of phosphorescence performance [36]. As shown in Fig. 3b and Fig. S15, the phosphorescence lifetime of NP-CDs and A-CDs drops sharply to 44.16 and 52.44 ms, respectively. Therefore, it is deduced that the vibration of phosphorescent emitters and sub-fluorophores on the surface of NP-CDs and A-CDs will be enhanced without restriction of covalent crosslinking [3,29,41,44]. So, it will enhance the non-radiative transition and weaken the phosphorescence. It was found that the non-radiative rates of NP-CDs (23.24 s−1) and A-CDs (34.15 s−1) were indeed larger than that of NP-CDs (4.00 s−1), proving the phosphorescence efficiency can be enhanced by covalent crosslinking in AP-CDs. Moreover, as shown in Fig. 3c and Fig. S16, the phosphorescence emission of as-prepared three CDs are located in the blue area with the corresponding emission peaks at 470, 440, and 474 nm, respectively and corresponding CIE coordinates of (0.196, 0.190), (0.197, 0.238), and (0.213, 0.295).

Based on above results, we proposed the possible formation process and bonding mode of covalent crosslinking structure [47–49]. As shown in Fig. 3d, during heating, the side of the APTMS molecule connected with Si hydrolyzes to generate Si–OH. Next, dehydration, condensation, and polymerization occur between adjacent Si–OH to form oligomers with SiOSi covalent bonds, or dehydration and condensation with one end of the carbon chains to form oligomers with SiOC and SiC covalent bonds. At the same time, the elimination reaction at the end of the carbon chains of the precursor molecules makes it possible to change the CN bond to the CN bond, which can be confirmed by the FT-IR and XPS of AP-CDs. Phosphoric acid, as a cross-linking agent, can cross-link at the end of adjacent carbon chains [29,41]. Finally, the oligomers and phosphoric acid will tangle to form a crosslinking structure, limiting the vibration and rotation of the CN groups and sub-fluorophores on the surface of AP-CDs. Notably, under the action of covalent crosslinking, the distance between functional groups is pulled into the finite space of the nanometer level, which promotes the formation of the hydrogen bond [41]. Above all, we believe that the formation of the covalent crosslinking structure provides a rigid environment for the chromophores on the surface of AP-CDs, thereby inhibiting non-radiative transitions of triplet excitons for boosting phosphorescence at room temperature (Fig. 3e).

Due to the excellent phosphorescent performance of AP-CDs, the potential applications in information encryption were demonstrated. As shown in Fig. 4 a, we have selected a model composed of four numbers. The blue part of the model is filled with AP-CDs, and the green part is filled with interfering CDs that only emit blue fluorescence. Under the excitation of 365 nm, the model displays blue “8888”. After the excitation light source is removed, the encrypted digital information “2023” can be displayed clearly. Similarly, in the words of model “CDs”, the “CD” is filled with AP-CDs, while “s” is filled with interfering CDs. Under the excitation of 365 nm, the words of blue “CDs” appear. The correct information “CD” is displayed only with the removal of the excitation light source. Moreover, as shown in Fig. 4c, the kitten except the beard is filled with interfering CDs, other parts of its body are all AP-CDs, which show different pattern information with the excitation of UV light on or off. Due to the long lifetime of the material, these changes can be easily observed by the naked eyes. In view of its low cost, environmental protection, and easy synthesis, the CD-based materials show great potential in the application of information encryption.

In summary, we report efficient blue phosphorescence of matrix-free carbon dots through a covalent crosslinking method. By forming a covalent network on the AP-CDs surface to limit the vibration and rotation of surface emitters, the non-radiative transition of triplet excitons is inhibited, and the blue phosphorescence emission is realized. Notably, the phosphorescence lifetime at 470 nm emission wavelength is 315.73 ms and the phosphorescence quantum yield can reach 12.4 %. Considering the long phosphorescence lifetime emission of AP-CDs, they are successfully used for information encryption and pattern anti-counterfeiting. This work may provide new views into the design of room temperature phosphorescent material systems of carbon dots.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21975120, 22371123), the Open Research Fund Program of Collaborative Innovation Center for Molecular Imaging of Precision Medicine (2020-ZD01 and 2020-MS03).

ce:section.ce:para: H X. Shi and H F. Shi conceived the experiment. H. X. Shi, Y Wu and H F. Shi prepared the manuscript. Y Wu was primarily responsible for the experiments. Y Yi and other remaining authors made contributions to the experimental tests in this paper. H X Shi, H F Shi, and Z An revised this manuscript. All authors contributed to the data analyses.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2023.146524.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: HuMulti-emissive room-temperature phosphorescent carbon dots@ZnAl2O4 composites by inorganic defect triplet-state energy transfer

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Time-dependent phosphorescence colors from carbon dots for advanced dynamic information encryption

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly efficient and stable deep-blue room temperature phosphorescence via through-space conjugation

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Multicolor ultralong room-temperature phosphorescence from pure organic emitters by structural isomerism

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Efficient room temperature phosphorescence carbon dots: Information encryption and dual-channel pH sensing

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The influence of the molecular packing on the room temperature phosphorescence of purely organic luminogens

sb:title.sb:maintitle: White light afterglow in carbon dots achieved via synergy between the room-temperature phosphorescence and the delayed fluorescence

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Zhu, J. Hu, Q. Hu,X. Zhang, Elena V. Ushakova, K. Liu, S. Wang, X. Chen, C. Shan, Andrey L. Rogach, X. Bai, White light afterglow in carbon dots achieved via synergy between the room-temperature phosphorescence and the delayed fluorescence, Small 18 (2022) 2105415, https://doi.org/10.1002/smll.202105415.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Functional IrIII complexes and their applications
